meta.title: Climbing Mountains: An Interview with Erik Weihenmayer
meta.author: Kathie Lester
meta.focusQuestion: How has Erik Weihenmayer overcome blindness to become a world-class mountain climber?

sections.title: About Erik
paragraphs: <s>Erik Weihenmayer, 36 years old,</s> <v>has climbed</v> <o>the Seven Summits, the highest peaks on each of the seven continents</o>. <s>He</s> <v>was born with</v> <o>a genetic condition that caused him to lose his sight by age 13</o>.

Interviewer: Can you see at all, or is everything black? Erik: It's your eyes that go blind, not your brain, so <s>I</s> <v>imagine</v> <o>an outline or shades of color</o>. If <s>I</s> <v>hear</v> <o>someone talking</o>, <s>I</s> <v>imagine</v> <o>a face and a body</o>.

Interviewer: Is it like how I picture people when I talk to them on the phone? Erik: Exactly. It's a fuzzy outline of things that my brain fills in so <s>I</s> <v>understand</v> <o>the shape of the world</o>.

Interviewer: How did you feel when you lost your sight? Erik: Like a raccoon that's been cornered—<s>I</s> <v>didn't know</v> <o>what to do or who to turn to</o>. I just panicked. <s>I</s> <v>didn't know</v> <o>what would happen to me as a result of being blind</o>, just that everything seemed harder. <s>I</s> <v>had</v> <o>a lot of fears</o>, and a lot of anger bubbled beneath the surface; but <s>I</s> <v>gradually realized</v> <o>I had to accept it</o>. Rock climbing was one of the ways I renewed myself.

sections.title: How Erik Learned to Climb
paragraphs: Interviewer: How did you learn to rock climb? Erik: At first <s>someone</s> <v>gave</v> <o>me detailed instructions</o> like "reach right" and "put your foot farther left," but now <s>I</s> <v>climb</v> much more independently by <v>feeling</v> <o>the cracks and pockets in the rock</o>.

Interviewer: What does mountain climbing mean to you? Erik: The scenery is exciting—the sounds, how the rock feels under my hands, the sun in my face, the ways the ground changes under my feet. It's exciting to <v>challenge</v> <o>the environment with my team, using my skill and endurance</o>, <v>bringing</v> <o>uncontrollable situations under control</o>.

Interviewer: How did you get started in climbing? Erik: <s>The Carroll Center for the Blind</s> <v>had</v> <o>an activities program for blind kids</o>. Blind kids can't participate in team ball sports, so when they took us rock climbing, <s>I</s> <v>knew</v> <o>I'd found something I could do that was really exciting</o>.

sections.title: Ice Climbing
paragraphs: When <s>I</s> <v>climb</v> <o>vertical ice</o>—frozen walls of ice that look like waterfalls—<s>I</s> <v>kick into</v> <o>the ice with crampons</o>, <o>which are sharp points on the toes of my boots</o>. <s>I</s> <v>stand up</v> high on the toes of my boots and <v>swing</v> <o>my ice tools into the ice</o>, and then <s>I</s> <v>hang from</v> <o>the ice tools</o> and <v>pull</v> <o>myself up</o>. I don't have to see where I'm swinging.

<s>Sighted climbers</s> <v>look for</v> <o>blue ice</o> because white ice is rotten, but <s>I</s> <v>can tell</v> if it's rotten by tapping the ice with the tools and listening to the sound and the vibration.

sections.title: How Erik Navigates
paragraphs: Interviewer: Please describe how you know where you're going when mountain climbing. Erik: When I'm hiking, <s>I</s> <v>follow</v> <o>somebody who has a bell tied to his pack</o>. <s>I</s> <v>use</v> <o>long trekking poles</o> to <v>feel</v> <o>the terrain in front of me</o>. When I find a place I want to step, I lean on that pole and take a step, and then I scan with the other pole. It sounds slow, but it's not; I can hike as fast as anyone.

When we go up nearly vertical snow in deep gullies called couloirs, <s>I</s> <v>kick</v> <o>the toes of my boots into the snow</o> and <v>take</v> <o>one step at a time</o>, and <s>I</s> <v>swing</v> <o>an ice ax into the snow</o>. I'm feeling my way up slowly just like everyone else.
Interviewer: Can you take the lead climbing on a mountain? Erik: <s>I</s> <v>can lead</v> <o>a climb on rocks</o> by touch when we're climbing a vertical crack or pockets.

Interviewer: But you don't lead when you're crossing areas where there are crevasses? Erik: <s>On very narrow snow bridges that go over crevasses</s>, <s>I</s> <v>would need</v> <o>to be able to see in order to line up the best route</o>. On a big mountain, I'm usually roped to two other people, and <s>I</s> <v>follow</v> <o>someone</o>.

sections.title: Funniest and Scariest Moments
paragraphs: Interviewer: What is the funniest thing that ever happened to you while climbing? Erik: About a half hour before we summited Mt. McKinley, <s>we</s> <v>radioed down</v> to the village. When <s>we</s> <v>reached</v> <o>the top</o>, <s>my dad, wife, and two brothers</s> <v>were flying</v> above us in a small plane. <s>We</s> <v>waved</v> <o>our ski poles</o>, all wearing red suits, looking identical to each other. My friend said, "Yeah, you're the only one waving your ski pole in the wrong direction."

Interviewer: What is the scariest thing? Erik: My idea of climbing is to keep it boring. <s>I</s>'ve <v>fallen</v> in <o>crevasses</o> when snow bridges have collapsed. Once when I was on a lead climb, <s>a piece of gear</s> <v>popped out of</v> <o>the rock</o>, causing me to fall about 25 feet and dangle at the end of my rope. <s>Once I</s> <v>was hanging</v> on <o>some ice</o> while <s>an avalanche</s> <v>poured down</v> <o>over my head</o>.

sections.title: Summiting Everest
paragraphs: Interviewer: Can you describe what it is like to be on top of Mount Everest? Erik: When <s>you</s> <v>get to</v> <o>the summit</o>, it's done—it's over. What's exciting to me is the planning, building a team, and getting prepared the best I can. <s>You</s> <v>stand</v> on the summit for 20 minutes at the most. On top of a mountain, the sound changes, because there's sky above and around you, and the sound vibrations move forever.
<s>Ninety percent of climbing accidents</s> <v>happen</v> on the way down. <s>Edmund Hillary, the first man to climb Mount Everest,</s> <v>said</v> <o>it only counts if you get down</o>.

sections.title: Guide Dogs and Other Sports
paragraphs: Interviewer: Do you have a guide dog? Erik: Yes, <s>I</s>'ve <v>had</v> <o>three German shepherds</o>. My first dog, when I was 16, was Wizard; my second, Seigo, is retired; and I've had my third dog, Willa, for about a year. If <s>I</s> <v>come out of</v> a movie theater and <v>say</v>, "Find the car," <s>she</s>'ll <v>bring</v> <o>me</o> right to our car when there are 100 cars in the parking lot.

Interviewer: Do you do other extreme sports? Erik: I tandem bike. <s>I</s>'ve <v>done</v> <o>adventure races</o>, where you mountain bike, kayak, raft, rock climb, hike, and canoe. In the Primal Quest, the toughest adventure race in the world, we were one of 42 teams to finish out of 80 teams that started.

sections.title: Tibet Trip
paragraphs: Interviewer: Tell me about your upcoming trip to Tibet. Erik: <s>A German lady named Sabriye Tenberken</s> <v>traveled to</v> <o>Tibet</o>, where <s>she</s> <v>found</v> <o>blind kids who were four years old and hadn't even learned to walk</o>. <s>She</s> <v>started</v> <o>a school for the blind that now teaches 39 kids who were outcasts six or seven years ago</o>. <s>Now they're</s> <v>walking</v> <o>with their canes through the city of Lhasa</o>, <v>learning</v> <o>Braille in three different languages</o>.

We thought it'd be cool to take six of her most fit, motivated kids up a peak called Lhakpa Ri that's just north of Mount Everest. <s>We</s> <v>trained</v> <o>them</o> for three weeks for a month-long trip. One day <s>we</s> <v>went over</v> <o>a big 17,500-foot mountain pass</o>. It was storming—one was throwing up, and one fell into a river. They all passed, so we think they're ready.

sections.title: Erik's Message
paragraphs: Interviewer: What's your message for other people who are facing difficult challenges? Erik: When <s>a blind person</s> <v>stands</v> on top of <o>a mountain</o>, <s>it</s> <v>makes</v> <o>people reconsider what they think is possible</o>. <s>People</s> <v>think</v> less about <o>what they can't do</o> and more about <o>what they might be able to do</o>.

I've learned from the mountains that you don't have to be the fastest, or the smartest, or the most popular, or the person with the best eyesight to be a leader. <s>You</s> <v>need</v> <o>some skill</o>, but <s>you</s> also <v>need</v> <o>a vision of who you are and what kind of person you want to be</o>. Then <s>you</s> <v>need</v> <o>a lot of courage to carry it through</o>.

<s>Climbers</s> <v>call</v> <o>it "taking the sharp end of the rope,"</o> which means actually getting out there and climbing. When I think about leadership, it's about taking the sharp end—<s>it</s> <v>shows</v> in <o>your decisions</o> rather than in your words.

vocabulary.definition: a large mass of snow and ice sliding down a mountain
vocabulary.sentence: <s>Once I</s> <v>was hanging</v> on <o>some ice</o> while <s>an avalanche</s> <v>poured down</v> over my head.

options: a type of boot worn by climbers on very cold winter days
a large mass of snow and ice sliding down a mountain slope
a deep crack in ice that forms in glaciers and frozen rivers
a climbing rope used to tie climbers together on the mountain

vocabulary.definition: a system of printing for the blind that uses raised dots for letters
vocabulary.sentence: <s>Now they</s> <v>are walking</v> with their canes through the city of Lhasa, <v>learning</v> <o>Braille in three different languages</o>.

options: a mountain peak located in the country of Tibet near Everest
a system of printing for the blind that uses raised dots for letters
a type of climbing gear used to scale vertical walls of ice
a guide dog breed specially trained to help blind people navigate

vocabulary.definition: open, deep gorges or gullies usually containing snow or ice
vocabulary.sentence: When we go up nearly vertical snow in deep gullies called couloirs, I kick the toes of my boots into the snow.

options: types of rope used by climbers to secure themselves on steep slopes
open, deep gorges or gullies usually containing snow or ice
sharp metal spikes attached to boots for walking on frozen surfaces
frozen waterfalls that climbers scale using ice tools and crampons

vocabulary.definition: metal spikes attached to the bottom of boots to allow firm walking on snow or ice
vocabulary.sentence: <s>I</s> <v>kick into</v> the ice with <o>crampons</o>, <o>which are sharp points on the toes of my boots</o>.

options: ice tools used for swinging into frozen waterfalls while climbing
metal spikes attached to boots for walking firmly on snow or ice
a type of guide dog specially trained to lead climbers on mountains
a mountain peak covered in ice and snow that is difficult to climb

vocabulary.definition: deep cracks, especially in glacier ice
vocabulary.sentence: <s>On very narrow snow bridges that go over crevasses</s>, <s>I</s> <v>would need</v> <o>to be able to see in order to line up the best route</o>.

options: types of snow that form on the surface of a frozen mountain
deep cracks, especially in glacier ice, that can be very dangerous
frozen waterfalls that climbers scale using special tools and gear
metal climbing gear used to protect climbers from falling on ice

vocabulary.definition: film that dramatically shows factual events
vocabulary.sentence: I often speak to schools and show our Everest documentary.

options: a type of mountain that is very tall and difficult to climb
a film that dramatically shows factual events that actually happened
a climbing competition where teams race to reach a mountain summit
a guide dog that helps a blind person navigate through the city

vocabulary.definition: a way of locating objects by sending out a sound and measuring how long it takes to bounce back
vocabulary.sentence: Echolocation helps Erik understand the shape of the world around him.

options: a way of locating objects by sending out a sound and measuring how long it takes to bounce back
a type of climbing boot designed for walking on frozen surfaces and very icy terrain
a mountain navigation system that uses special equipment and detailed maps to find routes
a guide dog command that is used to direct the dog to go in a certain specific direction

vocabulary.definition: the ability to make it through suffering and strain
vocabulary.sentence: It's exciting to challenge the environment with my team, using my skill and endurance.

options: the ability to make it through suffering and strain during difficult activities
a type of mountain boot designed for climbing in very cold conditions
the speed of climbing a mountain or finishing a long race or journey
a climbing technique used to scale vertical walls of rock and ice

vocabulary.definition: having to do with heredity and variation of living things
vocabulary.sentence: <s>He</s> <v>was born with</v> <o>a genetic condition that caused him to lose his sight by age 13</o>.

options: having to do with heredity and the variation of living things over time
related to mountains and the challenging terrain found on their slopes
a type of weather condition that affects climbers on high peaks
a climbing technique used to navigate difficult and dangerous terrain

vocabulary.definition: people who are rejected by society
vocabulary.sentence: <s>She</s> <v>started</v> <o>a school for the blind that now teaches 39 kids who were outcasts six or seven years ago</o>.

options: people who are rejected by society and treated as if they do not belong
expert mountain climbers who have reached the highest peaks in the world
guide dogs specially trained to help blind people navigate safely
famous leaders who have inspired many people around the entire world

vocabulary.definition: the characteristics of a piece of land
vocabulary.sentence: I use long trekking poles to feel the terrain in front of me.

options: a type of boot designed for hiking on rough and rocky surfaces
the characteristics of a piece of land, including its shape and features
a climbing rope used to tie climbers together on dangerous terrain
a mountain peak that can be seen from many miles away on clear days

vocabulary.definition: a rapid back and forth movement
vocabulary.sentence: I can tell if it's rotten by tapping the ice with the tools and listening to the sound and the vibration.

options: a type of rope used by climbers to secure themselves on steep surfaces
a rapid back and forth movement that can be felt or heard clearly
a mountain peak that shakes during an earthquake or avalanche
a kind of boot designed for walking on icy and frozen surfaces

sequenceEvents.event: Erik was born with a genetic condition that would cause progressive sight loss.
Erik lost his sight completely and felt like a cornered raccoon — panicked and angry.
Erik got his first guide dog, Wizard, a German shepherd.
Erik discovered rock climbing through the Carroll Center for the Blind activities program.
sequenceEvents.detail: Found rock climbing at Carroll Center

sequenceEvents.event: Erik climbed the Seven Summits — the highest peaks on all seven continents.
Erik summited Mount Everest (29,035 feet), the highest peak in the world.
Erik completed a five-day adventure race across Greenland.
Erik finished the Primal Quest, the toughest adventure race in the world (42 of 80 teams finished).
Erik got his third guide dog, Willa, because he had a young daughter, Emma.
Erik trained six blind Tibetan teenagers for three weeks to climb Lhakpa Ri (23,100 ft) near Everest.

questions.question: How does Erik's sense of touch help him?
questions.evidence: I climb much more independently by feeling the cracks and pockets in the rock when climbing. I can tell if ice is rotten by tapping the ice with the tools and listening to the sound and the vibration.
choices: He uses touch to feel cracks in rocks, sense ground changes when hiking, and detect vibrations in ice.
He can only use his sense of touch to tie knots when he is climbing.
He can only use his sense of touch to feel wind direction on the mountain.
He can only use his sense of touch to hold onto ropes during the climb.
questions.explanation: Erik uses touch for everything: feeling cracks and pockets in rock, sensing ground changes when hiking, and detecting rotten ice through vibration.

questions.question: What does endurance mean?
questions.evidence: <s>It</s> <v>is exciting</v> to <v>challenge</v> <o>the environment with my team, using my skill and endurance</o>.
choices: The ability to make it through suffering and strain during difficult activities.
Being able to climb mountains quickly without stopping to rest.
Being able to sense vibrations from objects by tapping on them with tools.
A special type of climbing boot designed for walking on ice and snow.
questions.explanation: Endurance means the ability to push through suffering and strain — both physical and mental.

questions.question: How does Erik's sense of hearing help him?
questions.evidence: I can tell if it's rotten by tapping the ice with the tools and listening to the sound and the vibration. Sighted climbers look for blue ice because white ice is rotten, but I can tell by the sound.
choices: He can identify rotten ice by tapping it and listening to the vibration and sound it makes.
He can hear the sound of ice waterfalls and use them to find his way on the mountain.
He can find cracks in rocks by listening carefully to the sounds they make.
He can tell if a snow bridge will break by the sound it makes when he steps on it.
questions.explanation: Erik taps the ice and listens to the sound and vibration to determine if it's rotten (white) or solid (blue).

questions.question: Why would 90 percent of all climbing accidents happen on the way down the mountain?
questions.evidence: Ninety percent of climbing accidents happen on the way down. You're tired, and you don't want to trip and fall down the mountain.
choices: The climbers are tired after reaching the summit and must be extra careful on the descent.
It is harder to climb down a mountain than it is to climb all the way up.
There are more dangers waiting on the descent side of the mountain.
Both tired climbers and more dangers going down contribute to more accidents on the descent.
questions.explanation: After summiting, climbers are exhausted and the descent has its own dangers — tired climbers are more likely to trip and fall.

questions.question: Why would Erik say he wants to keep climbing 'boring'?
questions.evidence: My idea of climbing is to keep it boring. I've fallen in crevasses when snow bridges have collapsed...
questions.explanation: Erik wants climbing to be 'boring' because boring means SAFE — no avalanches, no falls, no crevasse collapses. Excitement in climbing usually means danger!
opinionPrompts: How does this connect to Erik's experience with danger?

questions.question: What effect did Erik's climbing have on blind Tibetan teenagers?
questions.evidence: We think this project will create leaders out of these kids and give them a sense of their lives being full of possibilities. We also think it's a message to the world about what people are capable of.
questions.explanation: Erik believes climbing will transform blind Tibetan kids from outcasts into leaders, showing them — and the world — that blind people are capable of extraordinary things.
opinionPrompts: What message does this send to the world?

intro.title: Genetic
intro.content: Having to do with heredity — traits passed from parents to children. Erik's blindness was genetic.

intro.title: Sequence Events
intro.content: Signal words like 'first', 'then', 'after', 'by age 13' help you put events in order. Look for DATES and TIMES!

learning.title: Endurance
learning.content: The ability to keep going through suffering and strain. Erik needed endurance for long climbs!

iceclimbing.title: Crampons
iceclimbing.content: Metal spikes on boots for walking on ice. Erik kicks them into frozen waterfalls!

iceclimbing.title: Vibration
iceclimbing.content: A rapid back and forth movement. Erik taps ice and LISTENS to the vibration to find rotten ice!

navigation.title: Crevasses
navigation.content: Deep cracks in glacier ice. Erik follows others when crossing snow bridges over crevasses because he needs to SEE them!

navigation.title: Couloirs
navigation.content: Deep gullies filled with snow or ice. Erik kicks his boots into the snow to climb these!

moments.title: Avalanche
moments.content: A large mass of snow and ice sliding down a mountain. Erik was once pelted with ice from one!

everest.title: Past vs Present Tense
everest.content: 'Erik CLIMBED Everest' = past tense (already happened). 'Erik CLIMBS mountains' = present tense (still true now). Which tense does Erik use when talking about the past vs. today?

tibet.title: Outcasts
tibet.content: People rejected by society. The blind Tibetan kids were outcasts before Sabriye's school changed their lives!

tibet.title: Braille
tibet.content: A printing system for blind people using raised dots. Erik reads and writes Braille!
